label: ai
topic: Men who confide their trauma to you — loneliness and male vulnerability
text:
The first one was my neighbour, Carl. He caught me taking out the trash on a Tuesday evening and said, without preamble, as if we were resuming a conversation we had never started, "My wife left in March and I haven't told anyone." It was October.

Carl was sixty-three. He had a leaf blower he used with religious devotion and a pickup truck he washed every Sunday. He had never, in the four years I had lived next door, said anything to me more personal than a comment about the weather. Now he was standing in my driveway in the blue light of dusk, telling me that he had been eating dinner alone for seven months and that sometimes he set two places out of habit and sat across from the empty plate.

"I don't know why I'm telling you this," he said.

I think I do. I am a woman in her thirties who makes eye contact and does not interrupt, and this combination is apparently so rare in some men's lives that it functions as a confessional booth.

There is a statistic that I carry around like a stone in my pocket: men are significantly more likely than women to report having no close friends. The numbers have worsened steadily over the past three decades. One in five men reports having no close social connections outside of a romantic partner. When the partner leaves, the entire infrastructure of emotional support goes with her, like pulling a tablecloth and watching everything on the table crash to the floor.

Carl stood in my driveway for forty-five minutes. He told me about the leaf blower, which he had bought because the sound of it filled the yard and made the silence afterward feel intentional rather than imposed. He told me about his daughter, who called once a month. He told me he had tried joining a men's group at the community centre but left after one meeting because a man had cried and Carl had not known where to look.

"I'm not good at this," he said. He meant talking. He meant feeling. He meant standing in a driveway admitting to a woman he barely knew that he was lonely enough to set a place for a ghost.

The second one was my friend Patrick. We were at a bar, the kind of bar where the lighting is forgiving and the music is loud enough to provide cover for whatever you're about to say. Patrick had three beers in him, which was apparently the exact dosage required to unlock the part of his brain where he kept a memory from college — a night he had never told anyone about, involving an older student and a locked room and the word no, which he said he had used and which had not been effective.

"It's not a big deal," he said, which is the sentence men use to introduce the biggest deals of their lives.

Patrick told the story quickly, almost casually, with the detached efficiency of someone reading directions. He did not make eye contact. He peeled the label off his bottle in thin strips. When he finished, he said, "Anyway," and changed the subject to football, and the conversation closed over the wound like water over a dropped stone.

I wanted to say something adequate. I said, "I'm sorry that happened to you." He nodded once and finished his beer.

The third one was my father. This one I did not expect, although perhaps I should have. We were driving home from my aunt's funeral, the two of us, three hours of highway stretching ahead. My father drives with both hands on the wheel and his eyes fixed forward, which I had always interpreted as caution but now suspect was a way of not having to look at anyone while he spoke.

Somewhere around mile eighty, he told me that he had been depressed for most of his forties. Not sad. Depressed. The clinical kind, the kind with a weight and a colour, which he described as grey. He said he used to sit in his car in the driveway after work for twenty minutes before coming inside, because the transition from the silence of the car to the noise of the house required a kind of energy he did not have.

"I never told your mother," he said.

"Why not?"

"I didn't want to be a burden."

The mathematics of this are devastating. A man sits alone in a car, in a driveway, twenty feet from people who love him, because he has decided that his pain is an imposition. He has been taught, by a culture that is too cowardly to examine its own teachings, that needing help is a form of weakness, and that weakness in a man is a form of failure.

I think about Carl setting an empty place at the table. I think about Patrick peeling a label off a bottle. I think about my father in the driveway, the engine off, the house lit up in front of him, gathering the strength to walk thirty steps.

These men found me, or I found them, or we simply occupied the same space at the moment the silence became unbearable. They did not want advice. They did not want solutions. They wanted what everyone wants and what men are least likely to ask for: a witness.

I hear you. I hear all of you.
